title: VPN for Chromebook: Android App Limits, Linux Container Method, and Native Chrome OS Support
description: Chromebook VPN setup is more complicated than it looks. Android apps, Linux containers, and Chrome OS built-in VPN each work differently. Here's what actually protects all your traffic.
content:
## Why VPN on Chromebook Is More Complicated Than on Other Devices

On a Windows PC or Mac, you install a VPN app and it protects everything. On a Chromebook, the situation is more fragmented. Chrome OS runs three separate environments: the Chrome browser, an Android app container, and a Linux (Crostini) container. A VPN running in one environment does not automatically protect the others.

This guide explains what each method actually covers, which VPNs work natively with Chrome OS, and how to get full-device protection.

## Method 1: Android VPN Apps

Most Chromebooks sold since 2017 support Android apps via the Google Play Store. You can install NordVPN, ProtonVPN, ExpressVPN, or any major VPN as an Android app.

**What it covers:** The Android container and Chrome browser traffic. When you enable a VPN through an Android app on Chrome OS, the tunnel protects your web browsing and most system traffic.

**What it does not cover:** The Linux container (Crostini). If you run apps inside Linux on your Chromebook (terminals, development tools, servers), their traffic routes outside the VPN tunnel by default. This is a known Chrome OS limitation.

**How to install:**
1. Open the Google Play Store on your Chromebook
2. Search for your VPN of choice
3. Install and sign in
4. The Android app runs as a standard VPN on the system level

Android apps run in a compatibility layer, not natively. For most users doing regular browsing, streaming, and cloud-based work, this is fine. The VPN protects what matters most.

**One catch:** Some older Chromebooks have limited RAM, and running an Android VPN app alongside Chrome tabs can cause slowdowns. If performance is an issue, the built-in VPN client (Method 3) is lighter.

## Method 2: Linux Container (Crostini) VPN

If you use the Linux development environment on your Chromebook and want VPN protection inside it, you need to run a VPN inside the Linux container separately.

This is for advanced users. If you do not use the Linux environment, skip this section.

**Setup via Linux CLI:**

For ProtonVPN (which has a well-documented Linux CLI):
```
sudo apt update
sudo apt install proton-vpn-gnome-desktop
```

For WireGuard (manual config):
```
sudo apt install wireguard
```
Then import your WireGuard config file from your VPN provider's dashboard.

**The routing limitation:** Even with a VPN running inside Linux, and a separate VPN running at the Android-app level, you effectively have two separate VPN tunnels that do not stack cleanly. The Linux container has its own network namespace. Getting VPN protection in both Chrome OS and the Linux container simultaneously requires routing the Linux container through the Android VPN, which Chrome OS started supporting in newer versions but is not fully consistent across devices.

**Practical advice:** If you do development work in the Linux container and need VPN protection there, run your VPN directly in the Linux container using the CLI. Your browser traffic through Chrome OS will not be covered by that VPN, so you may want to run the Android app VPN simultaneously for the browser side.

## Method 3: Built-in Chrome OS VPN Client

Chrome OS has a built-in VPN client that supports OpenVPN and IKEv2/IPsec configurations. This is the lightest-weight option and protects native Chrome OS traffic without running an Android app.

**How to set it up:**
1. Go to Settings > Network > Add Connection > Add built-in VPN
2. Choose L2TP/IPsec or OpenVPN/L2TP
3. Enter your VPN server address, username, and password
4. You can also import an OpenVPN `.onc` config file via Chrome's certificate management

**Limitations:** Not all VPN providers support this cleanly. You need the raw OpenVPN or IKEv2 credentials, not just the app. NordVPN, ProtonVPN, and Mullvad all provide these credentials in their account dashboards, but it takes a few extra steps to find them.

The built-in client does not protect Android apps or the Linux container. It only covers native Chrome OS browser traffic.

## Which VPNs Have Native Chrome OS Support

**NordVPN:** Android app works well on Chrome OS. Built-in VPN config available via manual OpenVPN setup. Best all-around option.

**ProtonVPN:** Android app works on Chrome OS. Also has a Linux CLI client for the Crostini container. The only VPN with both an Android-layer option and a proper Linux container option.

**ExpressVPN:** Android app works. No separate Linux container app.

**Mullvad:** No Android app. You can use the built-in Chrome OS VPN with WireGuard or OpenVPN config files, or use the Linux container CLI. Good for privacy-focused users who know their way around a terminal.

**Surfshark:** Android app works on Chrome OS.

## What Actually Covers Everything

If you want full protection across Chrome browser, Android apps, and Linux container:

1. Use the Android app for Chrome browser and general traffic
2. Run ProtonVPN or a WireGuard config inside the Linux container for dev tools

Or, use a router-level VPN. If you set up WireGuard or NordVPN on your router at home, all traffic leaving your home network is encrypted regardless of device. This covers Chromebook traffic completely without any per-device setup.

## Recommendation

For most Chromebook users who want VPN for privacy and public Wi-Fi protection: install the **NordVPN Android app** from the Play Store. It takes two minutes, protects your browsing, and works reliably across Chrome OS versions.

For Chromebook users doing Linux development who need VPN coverage inside Crostini: use **ProtonVPN**, which is the only major provider with both an Android app and a documented Linux CLI client that works in Crostini.

Avoid VPN browser extensions as your primary method. They only encrypt traffic within the Chrome browser tab, not Android apps or anything else on the device.
